core.intro: Mulberries are one of the easiest and most rewarding fruit trees you can plant in Australia: tough, fast, almost pest-free, and heavy-bearing within a year or two. The state sections cover the climate, harvest timing and pests where you are. The notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Mulberries fall into two main camps. Black mulberries (Morus nigra and the dark-fruited types sold here as Black English, Hicks Fancy, Beenleigh Black and Lena) give the classic rich, sweet and slightly tangy berry on a smaller, garden-friendly tree. White mulberries (Morus alba, and the Shahtoot types Morus macroura sold as White Shahtoot and Red Shahtoot) carry very sweet, mild, low-acid fruit that barely stains, though the trees can grow large. Dwarf forms of the black mulberry are the pick for pots and small courtyards. Choose by flavour, by how much room you have, and by whether you want to avoid staining fruit near paving. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost. If you like mulberries, our fig guide covers another easy, fast-bearing tree from the same family.

sections.heading: One tree is enough
sections.body: You only need a single mulberry. They are wind pollinated, and most named varieties set a full crop on their own with no second tree and no separate pollinator, so one tree in a courtyard will fruit happily. Many cultivars set fruit with no pollination at all. That makes mulberries one of the simplest fruit trees to grow: plant one, and it crops.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give a mulberry full sun and a deep, free-draining soil. They are not fussy and will grow in most garden soils, from sand to heavy clay, but they do best in a deep loam and dislike shallow soils and waterlogging. Plant with room in mind, because a full-size mulberry becomes a wide, spreading tree unless you choose a dwarf or compact variety and keep it pruned. A little shelter from strong, salty coastal wind helps. Container-grown trees can go in for most of the year as long as you keep the water up through that first summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Watering, feeding and pruning
sections.body: Once established, mulberries are tough and drought-hardy, but they still reward watering through dry spells, which sizes and sweetens the fruit, so soak young and bearing trees deeply over summer. They are light feeders: an annual mulch of compost or well-rotted manure in late winter, topped up with a light dressing of a complete fertiliser as growth resumes in early spring, keeps them productive without pushing the soft, leafy growth that comes at the expense of fruit. Prune lightly only. Mulberries resent hard pruning, and cutting a tree back hard can kill it or remove the coming crop, so just thin crowded wood and shorten over-long branches after fruiting to keep the tree low enough to net and pick. Because the fruit often forms on the new season's growth, a light yearly tidy actually helps them bear.

sections.heading: Birds: the main challenge
sections.body: Birds are the number one problem with mulberries by a wide margin. The fruit ripens over several weeks and birds will visit a tree daily. Netting the whole tree is the only reliable way to get clean fruit, which is the best reason to keep your tree pruned low and compact. On a small tree you can bag individual fruiting branches (an old onion or orange bag tied over a branch works well) and tap the ripe fruit straight into the bag. Mulberries have very few other pests or diseases, so birds, not sprays, are what you plan around.

sections.heading: Harvest and the staining fruit
sections.body: Mulberries ripen from late spring into summer, a little fruit at a time over several weeks. Black and red types are ready when they are fully coloured, soft and come away easily; white and Shahtoot types are sweetest once they soften and begin to drop, so many growers shake the tree and catch the fruit on a sheet. The ripe fruit is fragile and very perishable, keeping only a few days even in the fridge, which is why you almost never see fresh mulberries in the shops. The dark juice stains hands, clothes and pale paving (a handy trick from the old growers: rubbing the juice of green, unripe mulberries onto stained hands lifts the stain).

sections.heading: Eating and storing
sections.body: Mulberries are best eaten fresh, warm off the tree. A glut is easy to use up: they make excellent jam, cordial, pies and sauces, and they freeze well spread on a tray. The sweet white and Shahtoot types also dry well and were traditionally dried as a source of sugar. Because the fresh fruit does not keep, plan to pick often through the season and process or freeze whatever you cannot eat straight away.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named mulberry grown from a cutting or grafted, rather than a cheap unnamed seedling. Mulberries strike readily from cuttings, so named varieties are widely sold and fruit quickly and to type, while a seedling is unpredictable and can take ten years to bear. Check the label for the variety name and the eventual size, and lean to a dwarf or compact type if space is tight. The in-stock table above lists the named mulberries for sale now and their prices.

faqs.q: How soon will a mulberry tree fruit?
faqs.a: Surprisingly soon. A tree bought from a nursery will often carry a few berries in its first year or two and crop well within three or four years. The slow exception is a tree raised from seed, which can take a decade to bear and gives unpredictable fruit, so it pays to start with a propagated, named plant.

faqs.q: How big does a mulberry get, and can I grow one in a pot?
faqs.a: It depends on the type. White mulberries can become very large trees, while black mulberries stay smaller and take pruning well, so they are easily held to three or four metres for a backyard. For a balcony or courtyard, choose a dwarf black mulberry or a Shahtoot in a large pot, water and feed it regularly, and prune it each year to keep it compact.

faqs.q: Do mulberries lose their leaves in winter?
faqs.a: Yes. Mulberries are deciduous, dropping their leaves in autumn and standing bare through winter before leafing out again in spring and fruiting on the fresh growth. In warm, frost-free districts they hold their leaves longer and lose them only briefly. The bare winter tree is the right time for any light pruning.

They are about the easiest fruit tree there is. A mulberry needs full sun, an occasional deep soak in dry weather, a yearly mulch, and a net at fruiting time for the birds. There is usually nothing to spray for, no pollinator to organise and no fussy pruning, which is why a single tree can crop for decades with very little attention.

WA.intro: Growing mulberries in Western Australia. Mulberries are an old, dependable WA garden tree. They thrive from Perth courtyards to Wheatbelt farms and the warm Gascoyne, and they ask very little once they are established.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south-west's warm, dry, Mediterranean climate suits mulberries well. They need no winter chill to fruit, take the summer heat in their stride, and are very drought-tolerant once established, though they size and sweeten their fruit far better with a deep soak through the dry months. In the warm Gascoyne around Carnarvon they grow fast and fruit early.

sections.heading: Where mulberries grow in WA
sections.body: The mulberry is a classic backyard and homestead tree right across the south-west. You will find old, heavy-bearing trees in the Swan Valley, through the Perth Hills, and in Wheatbelt towns, and they are grown in the warmer Gascoyne and Mid West as well.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's warm spring brings the fruit on early. In Perth and the south-west the main flush runs from about October into December, and the warm Gascoyne ripens earlier still. Pick often, because the fruit comes a little at a time over several weeks.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Birds are the main and often the only problem, so plan to net. Mulberries carry very few pests or diseases here. Mediterranean fruit fly is endemic across WA, from Esperance to Carnarvon, but it prefers figs and stone fruit, with mulberry well down its list, so birds remain your real concern.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must meet import conditions, and many eastern nurseries will not post to WA at all. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here, which is why WA-grown stock and a handful of certified interstate nurseries make up most of the list.

faqs.q: Which mulberry suits a small Perth garden?
faqs.a: For a compact, heavy-bearing tree, a dwarf black mulberry or a Hicks Fancy is hard to beat, and both take pruning well to stay courtyard-sized. If you want a tree near a path or paving, plant a White Shahtoot instead, because its pale fruit barely stains.

faqs.q: Do mulberries cope with Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes, they are among the toughest fruit trees for Perth sand. Dig in compost when planting, mulch thickly, and use a soil wetting agent if the sand has turned water-repellent. Give the tree a deep soak every week or two through summer and it rewards you with plump, sweet fruit.

QLD.intro: Growing mulberries in Queensland. Mulberries are one of the easiest and most productive backyard fruit trees in the state, cropping heavily from the subtropical south-east to the tropical north. The Beenleigh Black is a popular Queensland-grown black mulberry.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's warm, frost-free climate is ideal. Mulberries grow fast and crop heavily, and they shrug off the summer humidity that troubles many other deciduous fruit trees. They grow from the subtropical south-east all the way to the tropical north, where vigorous types such as Lena do particularly well.

sections.heading: Where mulberries grow in QLD
sections.body: Mulberries are a backyard staple across the south-east, from Brisbane through the Lockyer Valley to the Sunshine Coast, and they are grown north to the Atherton Tableland. Old trees crop for decades with almost no care.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland sees the earliest mulberries in the country. In the warm south-east the main flush runs from about October into December (the Beenleigh Black and Hicks Fancy ripen in October and November), and trees in the tropical north can start even earlier.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Birds are the headline problem, so net your tree. Mulberries are otherwise remarkably trouble-free even through the humid summers. Queensland fruit fly is endemic and most active in warm, humid weather after summer rain, and it will use soft, ripe mulberries as a secondary host when its preferred fruits are scarce, so pick promptly and net the tree.

faqs.q: Which mulberry is best for a Queensland backyard?
faqs.a: The Beenleigh Black and Hicks Fancy are both excellent in the Queensland warmth, cropping heavily on a manageable, prunable tree. If you have more room and want very sweet, low-staining fruit, a White Shahtoot is a fine choice and handles the heat well.

faqs.q: Do mulberries need winter chill to fruit in Queensland?
faqs.a: No. Mulberries have no winter-chill requirement, which is exactly why they crop so reliably in warm Queensland gardens where apples, cherries and many stone fruits struggle to flower. A sunny spot and a little summer water are all they ask.

NSW.intro: Growing mulberries in New South Wales. The mulberry is the classic Australian backyard tree, and NSW grows it everywhere, from the warm coast to the cold tablelands. Hicks Fancy is a long-time favourite here.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Mulberries suit the whole state. On the warm coast they crop early and easily; in the cooler tablelands they crop a few weeks later but just as reliably. Inland districts are fine too, as long as young trees are watered through their first few summers while they put down roots.

sections.heading: Where mulberries grow in NSW
sections.body: Old mulberries shade backyards right across NSW: through Sydney and the Hawkesbury, up into the Hunter Valley, out to the inland Riverina, and along the Northern Rivers. Few fruit trees are as widely planted in the state.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the warm coast the season starts early, from about late October, and runs through December. Cooler tableland gardens follow a few weeks later. As everywhere, pick little and often as the fruit colours up rather than waiting for it all at once.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Birds are the main problem statewide, so net your tree. Queensland fruit fly is established along much of the coast and in many inland districts, and it will use soft, ripe mulberries as a secondary host, so pick promptly where fruit fly is active. Diseases are rarely an issue.

faqs.q: Which mulberry for a Sydney backyard?
faqs.a: A black mulberry gives the classic rich, tangy flavour on a compact tree, and Hicks Fancy and Black English are both popular and crop heavily. If you would rather have fruit that does not stain paths and paving, plant a White Shahtoot, and keep any tree pruned low so you can net it against birds.

faqs.q: Which mulberry suits the cooler NSW tablelands?
faqs.a: All mulberries are hardy enough for the tablelands, because they need no winter chill and take frost well once established. A black type such as Hicks Fancy or Black English ripens reliably even where summers are cool. Give a young tree a sheltered spot for its first winter or two and it will soon be away.

VIC.intro: Growing mulberries in Victoria. Mulberries are fully hardy here and crop reliably right across the state, just later in the season than in the warmer states.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's warm, dry summers ripen mulberries well, and its cooler districts simply ripen their fruit a little later. The maritime Mornington Peninsula, the cool Yarra Valley and the southern coast all grow them happily, because a mulberry copes with cool, damp conditions far better than most warm-climate fruit trees.

sections.heading: Where mulberries grow in VIC
sections.body: Mulberries shade gardens across Victoria: through Melbourne's suburbs, north into the Goulburn Valley, around the Yarra Valley, along the Mornington Peninsula, and east through Gippsland. Many are decades-old trees that still crop heavily each year.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria ripens latest in the country. The main mulberry season runs from about December into January and February, because the cooler summer slows the fruit. In the warmest northern districts it can start a little earlier.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Birds are the main problem, so net your tree. Victoria has far less pest pressure on mulberries than the warm states, and diseases are rare. Queensland fruit fly has spread into parts of Victoria's warmer north, so gardeners there should pick ripe fruit promptly and net the tree, while most of the cooler south stays low-risk.

faqs.q: Which mulberry ripens best in Melbourne's cool summers?
faqs.a: A black mulberry such as Hicks Fancy or Black English is the safe pick, because they crop reliably even where summers are mild, and the fruit colours up well into the new year. White and Shahtoot types fruit too, though they are at their sweetest given a hot, sunny position against a north-facing wall.

faqs.q: Are mulberries hardy enough for Victorian frosts?
faqs.a: Yes. Established mulberries are very cold-hardy and need no winter chill, so they crop across cool central and southern Victoria without trouble. Only a very young tree needs any frost protection, and just for its first winter or two while it settles in.
